elements.sent-2411-001: Algorithmic analysis of Markov decision processes (MDP) and stochastic games (SG) in practice relies on value-iteration (VI) algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-002: Since the basic version of VI does not provide guarantees on the precision of the result, variants of VI have been proposed that offer such guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-003: In particular, sound value iteration (SVI) not only provides precise lower and upper bounds on the result, but also converges faster in the presence of probabilistic cycles.
elements.sent-2411-004: Unfortunately, it is neither applicable to SG, nor to MDP with end components.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we extend SVI and cover both cases.
elements.sent-2411-006: The technical challenge consists mainly in proper treatment of end components, which require different handling than in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-007: Moreover, we provide several optimizations of SVI.
elements.sent-2411-008: Finally, we also evaluate our prototype implementation experimentally to confirm its advantages on systems with probabilistic cycles.
elements.sent-2411-009: It is used in the state-of-the-art model checkers such as Prism \cite{prism4 and Storm \cite{Storm as the default method due to its better practical scalability, compared to strategy iteration or linear/quadratic programming \cite{practitionerguide,gandalf20 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The price to pay are issues with precision.
elements.sent-2411-011: Firstly, while other methods yield precise results, VI converges to the exact result only in the limit.
elements.sent-2411-012: Secondly, the precision of the intermediate iterations was until recently an open question.
elements.sent-2411-013: Given the importance of reliable precision in verification, many recent works focused on modifying VI so that the imprecision can be bounded, yielding a stopping criterion.
elements.sent-2411-014: Consequently, (i) the computed result is reliable, and (ii) the procedure can even terminate earlier whenever the desired precision is achieved.
elements.sent-2411-015: The main idea of SVI is to estimate the value by a geometric series describing the probabilities to (i)~reach the target in a given number of steps and (ii)~to remain undetermined in that number of steps.
elements.sent-2411-016: For instance, in the system depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:3states , SVI essentially computes that within $n$ steps, the probability to reach the target is $\svireach_n=r+pr+p^2r+\cdots+p^{n-1 r$.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Moreover, since the probability to stay in undetermined states (neither target, nor sink) is $\svistay_n=p^n$, it deduces that the infinite-horizon reachability probability is in the interval $[\svireach_n, \svireach_n+\svistay_n]$.
elements.sent-2411-018: Not only does the method provide such valid lower and upper bounds, but it also terminates (bounds are close enough to each other) comparatively faster when such probabilistic cycles are present, rendering the usual VI very slow~\cite{DBLP:conf/cav/QuatmannK18 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Unfortunately, SVI is only applicable to MDP without end components (ECs).
elements.sent-2411-020: While this is a common simplifying restriction (stochastic shortest path in MDP \cite{ssp , stopping games \cite{shapley ), it is also very severe.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we extend SVI (i) to SG and (ii) to systems (both MDP and SG) with end-components (ECs).
elements.sent-2411-022: First, we extend SVI to SG without ECs.
elements.sent-2411-023: While the algorithm is similar with analogously dual steps for the other player, the correctness is surprisingly not immediate.
elements.sent-2411-024: Indeed, the underlying optimization objective in SVI is not simple reachability but a non-trivial combination of step-bounded reachability of targets and of undetermined states.
elements.sent-2411-025: This in turns makes these strategies require memory; the interaction of two memory-dependent strategies then convolutes constraints on the upper bound.
elements.sent-2411-026: Second, we extend the approach to handle ECs.
elements.sent-2411-027: A major portion of the literature on guaranteed-precision VI, i.e.~with lower and upper bounds, typically considers only the case without ECs \cite{DBLP:conf/cav/QuatmannK18 ; for MDP, the ECs can be collapsed before the analysis, avoiding the issue, but not so for SG.}
elements.sent-2411-028: The main approach to treat ECs is deflating \cite{KKKW18 , which adjusts the upper bound of each state to reflect the true probability of reaching the target outside of the EC.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Since SVI does not work with the explicit upper bound, but rather with an implicit version given by other values (such as $\svireach_n$ and $\svistay_n$), deflating cannot be performed directly.
elements.sent-2411-030: Consequently, (i) instead of a single (S)VI update, we shall have four different cases to handle; (ii) the fundamental notion of the simple end component (EC states with the same value), instrumental for other VI extensions, is too coarse; instead, we have to consider their yet smaller parts; (iii) the convergence of the bounds does not follow directly from the literature.
elements.sent-2411-031: In particular, instead of the easy, standard convergence of the lower bound as seen in all other VI variants, we need properties of the upper bound to argue about the convergence of the lower bound.
elements.sent-2411-032: This constitutes the technically most involved part of the paper.
elements.sent-2411-033: After providing an instructive description of SVI (in particular adding some aspects lacking in the original \cite{DBLP:conf/cav/QuatmannK18 ), we extend SVI to (i) SG and then (ii) to handle ECs.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Besides, we apply the classical topological optimization to our approach, and we further improve it based on the performed in-depth analysis.
elements.sent-2411-035: Our~prototypical implementation and experimental analysis illustrate that our extension preserves the main advantage of SVI (see Sec.~\ref{svi-good-example for a concrete example)---fewer iterations are needed for systems with probabilistic cycles.}
elements.sent-2411-036: It should be emphasized that our main contribution is not showing that the implemented tool is more efficient than other ones in the literature, but rather the theoretically involved proof and way how SVI can be extended to handle end components.
elements.sent-2411-037: Specifically, our contribution lies in highlighting the inductive, attractor-like structure of MECs, which provides a theoretical foundation for addressing cycles, the primary challenge with VI.
elements.sent-2411-038: While this work does not aim to replace BVI, it offers critical insights into the inherent drawbacks of VI, paving the way for potential future enhancements to BVI with efficient cycle handling of SVI.
elements.sent-2411-039: This foundational step, though part of a longer-term effort, is pivotal for enabling subsequent advancements.. \myspace.

elements.subsection-2411-001: Value iteration and bounded value iteration
elements.sent-2411-060: Before we define the algorithm of value iteration, we recall a useful partitioning of the state space~\cite{DBLP:conf/cav/QuatmannK18 .}
elements.sent-2411-061: We know $\targets$ is the set of target states; w.l.o.g., every target state is absorbing.
elements.sent-2411-062: Similarly, we can identify by simple graph search the set $\sinks$ of sink states from which there is no path to $\targets$.
elements.sent-2411-063: The values of states in these sets are trivially 1 or 0, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-064: Our algorithm focuses on computing the values of the remaining states in $\unknown = \states \setminus (\targets \cup \sinks)$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Classical value iteration (VI, see~\cite{visurvey ) computes the following sequence $\lb_i:\states\to\mathbb{R}$ under-approximating the values. \begin{eqnarray}\lb_0(s) = \begin{cases} 1 \text{if} \ s \in \targets 0 \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \label{eqn:vi-updates} \lb_{i+1}(s) = \begin{cases} \max_{a \in \Av(s)} \sum_{s' \in \states} \distribution(s,a,s') \cdot \lb_i(s') \text{if} \ s \in \maxStates \min_{a \in \Av(s)} \sum_{s' \in \states} \distribution(s,a,s') \cdot \lb_i(s') \text{if} \ s \in \minStates \end{cases}\end{eqnarray} These under-approximations converge (in the limit) to the value $\val$, which also is the least fixpoint of Eq.~\eqref{eqn:vi-updates .}}

elements.sent-2411-066: While this approach is often fast in practice, it has the drawback that the imprecision $\val-\lb_i$ is unknown.
elements.sent-2411-067: To address this, one can employ bounded value iteration (BVI, also known as interval iteration~\cite{atva,hm18,KKKW18 ).}
elements.sent-2411-068: It additionally computes an over-approximating sequence $\ub_i$ which allows to estimate the imprecision as $\ub_i(s)-\lb_i(s)$.
elements.sent-2411-069: Naively, the sequence is initialized as $\ub_0(s) = 0$ for all $s \in \sinks$ and $\ub_0(s) = 1$ for all other states, and then updated using Eq.~\eqref{eqn:vi-updates again.}
elements.sent-2411-070: However, then $\ub_i$ converges to the greatest fixpoint of Eq.~\eqref{eqn:vi-updates which can differ from the least fixpoint $\val$; this happens if and only if end components are present.}

elements.sent-2411-075: More generally, on an EC in a maximizing MDP, one can create a fixpoint by assigning any number larger than the actual value to all states of the EC (and dually, a smaller number in a minimizing MDP).
elements.sent-2411-076: In MDP, there is a rather simple solution: the entire EC can be collapsed into a single state, keeping all the actions, but then removing all self-loops.
elements.sent-2411-077: This ensures a single fixpoint and, since all states in an EC have the same value as the collapsed state, it allows to obtain the values for the original MDP~\cite{atva,hm18 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: For SG, this solution is not applicable because states in an EC can have different values, see~\cite[Sec.. 3]{KKKW18 .}
elements.sent-2411-079: The solution of~\cite{KKKW18 is to repeatedly identify subsets of ECs that possibly, given the current estimates, have the property that all states have the same value; then, the over-approximation in these subsets is conservatively lowered (``deflated'') to the over-approximation of the best action exiting the subset.}

elements.subsection-2411-002: Sound value iteration in Markov chains
elements.sent-2411-082: Sound value iteration (SVI,~\cite{DBLP:conf/cav/QuatmannK18 ) was introduced as an alternative to BVI that is faster while still providing guarantees on its precision.}
elements.sent-2411-083: It computes for increasing $k$ the probability of reaching the target set $\targets$ within $k$ steps, denoted $\probability(\reach^{\leq k \targets)$, and the probability to stay within the unknown set $\unknown$ during the first $k$ steps, denoted $\probability(\stay^{\leq k \unknown)$ --- see Footnote~\ref{footnote:formalReachEtc for the formal definitions of the operators $\reach^{\leq k}$ and $\stay^{\leq k}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: From these two sequences, it computes under- and over-approximations of the value utilizing three core ideas.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Sound value iteration for stochastic games without end components} \label{alg:svi-sg-high-level} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require SG $\G$ (partitioned into $\unknown,\targets,\sinks$) and precision $\varepsilon>0$ \Ensure $\val' \colon \states \to \Reals$ such that $\abs{\val'(s)-\val(s)} \leq \varepsilon$ for all $s \in \states$ \State For $s\in \targets$ and all $k\in\Naturals_0$, $\svireach_s^k \gets 1$ and $\svistay_s^k \gets 0$ \label{line:svi:initStart} \State For $s\in \sinks$ and all $k\in\Naturals_0$, $\svireach_s^k \gets 0$ and $\svistay_s^k \gets 0$ \State For $s\in \unknown$, $\svireach_s^0 \gets 0$ and $\svistay_s^0 \gets 1$ \State $\glb_0\gets0$ and $\gub_0\gets1$ \label{line:svi:initEnd} \State $k\gets 0$ \Repeat \State Use ``$k$-step'' Strategies to choose $\sigma_{k+1}$ and $\tau_{k+1}$ -- see Eq.~\eqref{eq:strats} \label{line:svi:chooseStrats} \For{all $s\in\unknown$} \If{updateGlobalBounds? -- see condition~\eqref{eqn:updateGlobalBounds-no-ec}} \State Compute $\svireach_s^{k+1}$ and $\svistay_s^{k+1}$ by Bellman-Update -- see Eq.~\eqref{alg:update-no-ec} \label{line:svi:updateStepBoundProbs} \EndIf \EndFor \State Compute $\glb_{k+1}$ and $\gub_{k+1}$ by (Dynamic) Bounds -- see Eq.~\eqref{eq:def-bounds} \label{line:svi:updateGlobalBounds-l-u} \State $k \gets k+1$ \Until{$\svistay^k_s \cdot (\gub_k-\glb_k) < 2\cdot\varepsilon$ for all $s\in\unknown$} \label{line:svi:terminationCond} \State \Return $\svireach^k_s + \svistay^k_s \cdot \frac{\glb_k+\gub_k}{2}$ for all $s \in \unknown$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.section-2411-004: Extending sound value iteration to systems with end components
elements.sent-2411-155: In this section, we first present an example demonstrating that Alg.~\ref{alg:svi-sg-high-level does not converge in the presence of ECs.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Second, we demonstrate that while the natural extensions of the deflate solution from the literature~\cite{KKKW18 address the issue for BVI, they are no solution to SVI.}
elements.sent-2411-157: Third, we introduce new notions of best-exit set (BES) and delay action, based on which we design and prove a valid solution.
elements.sent-2411-158: Finally, we provide an example to showcase that the main advantage of SVI are preserved even in SG.
elements.subsection-2411-006: Approximations do not converge in presence of ECs
elements.sent-2411-159: Consider, for instance, the example of Fig.~\ref{fig:example1 with $\unknown=\{s\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-160: We apply Alg.~\ref{alg:svi-sg-high-level to this example.}
elements.sent-2411-161: For $k=0$, the initialization $\glb=0$ and $\gub=1$ correctly define the lower and upper bounds on optimal probability.
elements.sent-2411-163: For $k=1$, the algorithm picks $\sigma(s):=a$, resulting in no change to the approximations.
elements.sent-2411-164: This is true for all the subsequent iterations ($k=2,3,$...) as well.
elements.sent-2411-165: In particular, the over-approximation is always 1 and does not converge to the true $\nicefrac12$.
elements.sent-2411-166: The key element of any solution to the EC issue is to identify where to best exit the EC, which provides realistic information on the value compared to non-informative cyclic dependencies within the EC.
elements.subsection-2411-007: A sequence of difficulties: Why the natural solutions do not work
elements.sent-2411-167: ECs were previously handled for BVI, using so-called deflating~\cite{KKKW18 .}
elements.sent-2411-168: This approach finds simple end components (SEC), which are ECs with all states having the same value.
elements.sent-2411-169: In each SEC, deflating reduces the individual over-approximations for each state in the SEC to the value of Maximizer's best exit from the SEC (see Def.~\ref{def:bestExit ), since none of these states can possibly get any higher value.}
elements.sent-2411-170: In contrast, the difficulty with SVI is that it does not maintain explicit over-approximations for each state which could be reduced; instead, it computes over-approximations from the $k$-step values $\svireach$, $\svistay$ and $\gub$.
elements.sent-2411-171: Consequently, combining SVI with deflating is surprisingly involved.
elements.sent-2411-172: In the following we list the issues and have moved the detailed descriptions of the respective examples in App.~\ref{app:example_sec4 in the interest of space.}
elements.sent-2411-173: Although SVI maintains an over-approximation $\gub$, it is a global bound for all states and thus cannot be used to locally deflate the value of some states.
elements.sent-2411-174: SVI maintains $\svireach$ and $\svistay$, from which the local over-approximation for each state is derived.
elements.sent-2411-175: However, since the over-approximation depends on both variables, the same over-approximation can correspond to different pairs of these values.
elements.sent-2411-176: Consequently, when deflating a state, it is unclear whether to deflate reach or stay; moreover, there may not even exist any choice that works correctly for the whole EC (or SEC).
elements.sent-2411-177: Deflating only the best exit states of a SEC may result in non-convergence of the other states within the SEC.
elements.sent-2411-178: Even more complex is the difficulty with $\svireach$ and $\svistay$ being defined for a number $k$ of steps.
elements.sent-2411-179: While collapsing ECs results in not counting steps within ECs at all, the more complex handling of the inside of the EC results in a specific form of counting the steps.
elements.sent-2411-180: As a result, we shall introduce a ``delay'' action (see Sec.~\ref{ssec:delayExample ), which does not move us from the current state, yet it counts as making a step.}
elements.sent-2411-181: To summarize, we cannot deflate the entire SEC to the best exit of SEC because we might wrongly increase the $\svireach$ of a Minimizer state that may lead to incorrect results; and, we cannot only deflate the best exiting state $\bestExit_f^\Box$ as it may lead to non-termination.
elements.sent-2411-182: Note that the difficulties (1) and (3) highlighted in this subsection already arise in MDP.
elements.sent-2411-183: Our solution will have to decompose the ECs in even a finer way, into a bijection of the parts to the set of ``best exits''.

elements.sent-2411-198: The non-termination arises because the algorithm does not ensure monotonicity and becomes mired in cyclic dependencies with the ``missing'' probability oscillating between states (as observed between states $s_0$ and $s_1$ in this example).
elements.sent-2411-199: To address this, enforcing monotonicity proves effective as it guarantees that at least one state progresses according to the best-exit set, called $\bestExitSet$ in Sec.~\ref{ssec:bes .}
elements.sent-2411-200: To facilitate monotonicity, we introduce `delay' actions in Sec.~\ref{ssec:new-notions .}
elements.sent-2411-201: We propose a new approach that updates the vectors based on best exits of ECs using MEC decomposition and the idea of recursive deflate with a condition to enforce monotonicity of upper bounds.
elements.sent-2411-202: This method is also within the spirit of SVI, i.e. does not require to maintain local $\gub$ for every state.
elements.sent-2411-203: Our solution can be seen as Alg.~\ref{alg:svi-sg-high-level , where the computation of the strategies and bounds are adjusted by the re-defined procedures described in the following subsections.}
elements.sent-2411-204: As discussed in the previous section, it is crucial to determine the best exit from ECs.
elements.sent-2411-205: However, in different parts of the EC, there may be different answers to that question.
elements.sent-2411-206: Our approach is to identify a comprehensive set of the different best exits, each corresponding to some EC.
elements.sent-2411-207: Note that ECs that cannot be exited at all will not correspond to any best exit.
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Compute best exit set for the sound value iteration} \label{alg:bestexitset-high-level} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require an SG $\G$, over-approximation $f\geq \val$, an EC $\mec$ \Ensure Set of correct exiting state-action pairs of ECs in $\mec$ wrt $f$ \Procedure{BestExitSet}{$\G, f, \mec$} \State BES $\gets \emptyset$ \Comment{Initialization} \If {$\bestExit^\Box_f(\mec)$ is empty} \label{line:BES-hl:BestExitEmpty}\Comment{Base case} \State Include all the states of $\mec$ into $\sinks$ and exclude them from $\unknown$ \label{line:BES-hl:removeTrapECs} \State \Return BES \label{line:BES-hl:returnBaseCase} \Else \Comment{Recursive step: {$\bestExit^\Box_f(\mec)$ is non-empty}} \State Include all the best-exits of $\mec$ to BES \label{line:BES-hl:insertBE} \State Find MECs in the induced graph after removing all the states of the best-exits \label{line:BES-hl:MECsInInducedGraph} \State Recursively compute the best-exit set for all MECs obtained in the previous step and include them in BES \label{line:BES-hl:callBESOnAllMECs} \EndIf \State \Return BES {\Comment{Set of correct exits wrt $f$ for all EC within $\mec$}}\label{line:BES-hl:return2} \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-217: To find out in which state we need to play a delay action, we first introduce a condition `improvedUB?($s$)' that prevents the Maximizer state to update the approximation to worse values than those of current estimates (monotonicity): \begin{equation}\label{eqn:impUB} \boxed{ \begin{aligned} improvedUB?(s):= \svireach^{k+1}_s+\svistay^{k+1}_s \cdot \gub_k \leq \svireach^k_s+\svistay^k_s \cdot \gub_k \end{aligned} }\end{equation} The following condition then captures when to delay rather than to play another action. \begin{equation}\label{eq:delayaction} \boxed{ \begin{aligned} delayAction?(s) := s \in \unknown_{\Box} \land \lnot ImprovedUB?(s) \end{aligned} }\end{equation} Intuitively, if a Maximizer state $s$ is not a best exit of an EC and the upper bound for $s$ does not improve, we play a delay action which we denote as $d$.
elements.sent-2411-218: In particular, under the condition delayAction$(s)?$, the approximations for state $s$ at iteration $k+1$ are restored to the values from the $k$th iteration as detailed in the new part of Eq.~\eqref{alg:update-ec : \begin{equation}\label{alg:update-ec} \boxed{ \begin{aligned} \textsc{Bellman-Update ($\mathcal{B}$):} if~{delayAction?(s)} \triangleright New part to handle ECs \svireach^{k+1}_s \gets \svireach^{k}_{s};~~~\svistay^{k+1}_s \gets \svistay^{k}_{s} else \triangleright Same as before \svireach^{k+1}_s \gets \sum_{s' \in \states}\distribution(s,\pi_{k+1}(s), s') \cdot \svireach^{k}_{s'};~~~ \svistay^{k+1}_{s} \gets \sum_{s' \in \states}\distribution(s,,\pi_{k+1}(s), s') \cdot \svistay^{k}_{s'} \end{aligned} }\end{equation} Finally, we can re-define the strategies $\sigma_k$, reflecting the two new cases of exits and delays (while $\tau_k$ stays the same): \begin{equation}\fontsize{8.1}{11}\selectfont \label{eqn:newstrat} \boxed{ \begin{aligned} ``$k$-step'' Maximizer's contracting Strategies: \sigma_0(\rho) \in \Av(\rho_0) \sigma_{k+1}(\rho) \gets \begin{cases} \displaystyle \sigma_k(\rho') if \rho = sa\rho' d if \rho= s \land delayAction?(s) b if \rho= s \land (s,b)\in \bestExitSet^k \displaystyle\in\argmax_{a\in\Av(s)} \sum_{s' \in S} \distribution(s, a, s') \cdot \Big(\probability^{\sigma_k, \tau_k}_{s'} (\reach^{\leq k}\targets) + \probability^{\sigma_k, \tau_k}_{s'} (\stay^{\leq k}\targets) \cdot \gub_k \Big) otherwise \end{cases} \end{aligned} }\end{equation}.}

elements.section-2411-005: Topological variants of sound value iteration
elements.sent-2411-254: The core idea of topological value iteration~\cite{TVI1 is to exploit the fact that there is an ordering among states, and certain states can never be reached again.}
elements.sent-2411-255: More formally, a transition system can be decomposed into a directed acyclic graph of strongly connected components (SCC); the topological variant of a solution algorithm proceeds backwards through this graph, solving it component by component, see~\cite[Sec.~4.4]{MaxiGandalf-journal for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-256: Also, we note that using the standard topological approach can in practice lead to non-termination~\cite[Sec.~4]{atva22ovi-tvi .}
elements.sent-2411-257: We propose a new approach of exploiting the topology of the transition system, specialized for SVI.
elements.sent-2411-258: The strength of SVI is in picking good bounds $\glb$ and $\gub$ quickly.
elements.sent-2411-259: However, the bounds are picked as the minimum/maximum over all states in $\unknown$; thus, as long as there is one state that does not yet have a lot of information, it worsens the bounds for all others.
elements.sent-2411-260: In particular, if there is one state with a staying probability of 1, the bounds cannot be updated at all.
elements.sent-2411-261: These bad bounds additionally slow down convergence because the choice of strategies depends on the bounds.
elements.sent-2411-262: To address this problem, we propose to modify the definition of the bounds, in particular the term $\opt_{s \in \unknown ~ \frac{\svireach_s^k {1-\svistay_s^k}$ in Eq~\eqref{eq:def-bounds , as follows: instead of globally defining the bounds by picking the optimum among all unknown states, we define a bound for each state $s$ which only picks among those states that are reachable from $s$.\footnote{Formally, let $\mathsf{All\_reachable\_states (s)$ be the set of states reachable from $s$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-263: Then the bounds for $s$ use the term $\opt_{s \in \unknown \cap \mathsf{All\_reachable\_states (s) ~ \frac{\svireach_s^k {1-\svistay_s^k}$} This is correct because the bound represents the lowest/highest possible value the state $s$ can achieve after staying for $k$ steps; and, naturally, from $s$, the play can only reach a state that is reachable from $s$.}}
elements.sent-2411-264: In practice, we do not need to store a bound for every state, but instead one per SCC, because all states in the same SCC can reach each other, and thus also share their set of reachable states.
elements.sent-2411-265: This improvement allows for more informed choices in parts of the state space where $\svireach$ and $\svistay$ already suffice to derive good bounds, without being hampered by other slow or uninformed parts of the state space.
elements.sent-2411-266: Moreover, we can stop updating an SCC when its lower and upper bound are equal, saving some resources.
elements.sent-2411-267: We have extended the sound value iteration from Markov decision processes to stochastic games.
elements.sent-2411-268: In order to achieve that we had to lift the key assumption requiring that there are no end components.
elements.sent-2411-269: While the literature already suggests approaches to do so for other variants of value iteration, we have shown that they do not apply to sound value iteration.
elements.sent-2411-270: Instead we had to design a new dedicated solution.
elements.sent-2411-271: Besides, we have proposed improvements exploiting the topological properties of the models.
elements.sent-2411-272: While the approach is mostly on par with other value iteration approaches providing guarantees on precision, there are some examples where significant speed ups can be achieved.
elements.sent-2411-273: Yet, the point here was not a faster tool, but understanding the structure, in particular principles of evaluating cycles in SG.
elements.sent-2411-274: The inductive, attractor-like structure of MECs provides a key theoretical foundation for handling cycles.
elements.sent-2411-275: Furthermore, it addresses a critical drawback of VI and opens the path for potential future enhancements to BVI through efficient probabilistic cycle handling.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Sound value iteration for SG} \label{alg:svi-sg} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require SG $\G$ and naive precision $\varepsilon$ \Ensure $\val'$ such that $\diff(\val'(s),\val(s)) \leq \varepsilon$ for all $s \in \states$ \Procedure{SVI}{$\G, \varepsilon$} \State $\triangleright$ Initialization of $\svireach,\svistay,\glb,\gub,\svidecMin,\svidecMax$ \label{line:svi:init} \Repeat \State $\bestExitSet$ = HandleECs ($\G, \svireach, \svistay, \gub$) \label{line:svi:handleECs} \For{$s \in \unknown$} \State $\alpha \in \ FindAction(\svireach, \svistay, \glb, \gub, s, \bestExitSet)$ \label{line:svi:findActionCall} \Comment{pick arbitrarily among best actions} \State $\svidec \gets DecisionValue(\svireach, \svistay, s, \alpha)$ \label{line:svi:decValProcCall} \If {$s \in \maxStates$} \Comment{state belongs to Maximizer} \State $\svidecMax \gets \max(\svidecMax, \svidec)$ \label{line:svi:decVal-max} \Else \Comment{state belongs to Minimizer} \State $\svidecMin \gets \min(\svidecMin, \svidec)$ \label{line:svi:decVal-min} \EndIf \State Bellman-Update ($s, \alpha, \svireach,\svistay$) \label{line:svi:updateCall} \Comment{batch update using old values} \EndFor \If {updateGlobalBounds?} \label{line:svi:updateGlobalBounds} \Comment{depends on the presence of ECs and $\svistay$} \State $\glb \gets \max\Big(\glb, \min\big(\svidecMin, \min_{s\in\unknown}\geometricSeries{s}\big)\Big)$ \label{line:svi:updateglb} \State $\gub \gets \min\Big(\gub, \max\big(\svidecMax, \max_{s\in\unknown}\geometricSeries{s}\big)\Big)$ \label{line:svi:updategub} \EndIf \Until{$[\svistay_s \cdot (\gub - \glb)] < 2 \cdot \varepsilon$ for all $s \in \unknown$} \label{line:svi:terminationCheck} \item[] \State \Return $\svireach_s + \svistay_s \cdot \frac{\glb+\gub}{2}$ for all $s \in \unknown$ \label{line:svi:epsilonCloseVal} \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Find action computation for SG} \label{alg:svi:findAction} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Procedure{FindAction}{$\svireach, \svistay, \glb, \gub, s$} \If {$s \in \maxStates$} \State \Return $\argmax_{\alpha \in Act(s)} \Sigma_{s' \in \states} \distribution(s,\alpha, s') \cdot \left( \svireach_{s'}^k + \svistay_{s'}^k \cdot \gub \right) $ \label{line:findAction:maxAction-no-ec} \Else \State \Return $\argmin_{\alpha \in Act(s)} \Sigma_{s' \in \states} \distribution(s,\alpha, s') \cdot \left( \svireach_{s'}^k + \svistay_{s'}^k \cdot \glb \right)$ \label{line:findAction:minAction-no-ec} \EndIf \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{Decision value computation for SG} \label{alg:svi:decValue} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Procedure{DecisionValue}{$\svireach, \svistay, \mathit{s}, \alpha$} \State $ \svidec \gets \begin{cases} 0, \text{if~} s \in \maxStates 1, \text{if~} s \in \minStates \end{cases}$ \item[] \For{$\beta \in \mathit{Act(s)} \setminus \{\alpha\}$} \State $\Delta_{\svistay} \gets \Sigma_{s' \in S}(\distribution(s,\alpha, s') - \distribution(s, \beta, s')) \cdot \svistay_{s'}$ \item[] \If {$\Delta_{\svistay}$ is positive} \State $\Delta_{\svireach} \gets \Sigma_{s' \in S}(\distribution(s,\beta, s') - \distribution(s, \alpha, s')) \cdot \svireach_{s'}$ \State $ \svidec \gets \begin{cases} \max(\svidec, \Delta_{\svireach}/\Delta_{\svistay}), \text{if~} s \in \maxStates \min(\svidec, \Delta_{\svireach}/\Delta_{\svistay}), \text{if~} s \in \minStates \end{cases}$ \EndIf \EndFor \State \Return $\svidec$ \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}

elements.algorithm-2411-006: \caption{Compute best exit set for the sound value iteration} \label{alg:bestexitset} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require SG $\G, \svireach, \svistay, f,$ EC $\mec$ \Ensure Set of pairs of best exit states and actions of ECs \Procedure{BestExitSet}{$\G, f, \mec$} \State BES $\gets \emptyset$ \If {$\bestExit^\Box_f(\mec) = \emptyset$} \label{line:BES:bestExitEmpty} \Comment{$\bestExit^\Box_f(\mec)$ is empty} \State $\sinks = \sinks \cup \mec$ \Comment{Add all the states of $\mec$ to $\sinks$} \label{line:BES:addInSink} \State $\unknown = \unknown \setminus \mec$ \Comment{Remove all the states of $\mec$ from $\unknown$} \label{line:BES:removeFromUnknown} \State \Return BES \label{line:BES:return1} \Else \Comment{$\bestExit^\Box_f(\mec)$ is non-empty} \label{line:BES:bestExitNotEmpty} \State BES = BES $\cup \ \bestExit^\Box_f(\mec)$ \label{line:BES:insertBE} \Comment{Add all best exits to BES} \State $\mathsf{E}= \lbrace s\ |\ (s,a) \in \bestExit^{\Box}_f(\mec)\rbrace$ \label{line:BES:exitStates} \For{$M \in FindMecs(\G, \mec\setminus \mathsf{E})$} \label{line:BES:MECsInInducedGraph} \State BES = BES $\cup$ BestExitSet($\G, f, M$) \label{line:BES:callBESOnAllMECs} \EndFor \EndIf \State \Return BES \Comment{Set with best exits wrt $f$ for all the relevant ECs}\label{line:BES:return2} \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic}

elements.section-2411-009: Evaluation of prototype implementation of SVI
elements.sent-2411-582: We extended PRISM-games~\cite{prismgames3 with our prototype implementation of SVI for SG.}
elements.sent-2411-584: For comparison we used the implementation of standard bounded value iteration (BVI) with deflating~\cite{KKKW18 in PRISM-games.}
elements.sent-2411-585: For detailed comparison of algorithms for solving stochastic games such as value iteration (VI), optimistic VI (VI), quadric programming (QP), and strategy iteration (SI), please see a recent work~\cite{atva22ovi-tvi .}
elements.sent-2411-586: We ran our experiment on a Linux machine with 32GB RAM and Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-10750H 2.60GHz CPU with a timeout of 15 minutes and a memory limit of 5 GB.
elements.sent-2411-587: For all the expermients, we used a precision of $\varepsilon=10^{-6}$.
elements.sent-2411-588: For our experiments, we included the following case-studies:.
elements.sent-2411-589: All real benchmarks from \cite{atva22ovi-tvi which subsumes case studies from \cite{PRISMben .}}
elements.sent-2411-590: Along with the handcrafted models used in~\cite{gandalf20 , we also included three examples that we used to get in-depth understanding of SVI.}
elements.sent-2411-591: We picked all MDP benchmarks with a reachability objective from the quantitative verification benchmark set (QVBS)~\cite{QVBS .}
elements.sent-2411-592: We compare the number of iterations for statistics, as the strength of SVI lies in obtaining good global bounds and resolving probabilistic loops faster.
elements.sent-2411-593: It is crucial to note that our evaluation does not prioritize time comparison.
elements.sent-2411-594: This is because of the preliminary nature of our prototype implementation, which does not emphasize a well-engineered BES computation.
elements.sent-2411-595: While BVI needs to identify the complex structure within ECs, SVI requires involved MEC decompositions.
elements.sent-2411-596: Therefore, we anticipate that the overhead would be negligible for a well engineered implementation.
elements.sent-2411-597: How to read our results? We use scatter plots for comparison of SVI with BVI~\cite{KKKW18 .}
elements.sent-2411-598: A point on a scatter plot shows comparison of two algorithms shown on X- and Y-axis.
elements.sent-2411-599: A point above the diagonal indicates that the algorithm on X-axis is faster, a point below the diagonal shows that the algorithm on Y-axis is faster, and a point on the diagonal means both algorithms take the same number of iterations.
elements.sent-2411-600: Fig.~\ref{fig:comparisonSVI2BVI presents scatter plots that compare the number of iterations required by SVI and BVI for real and hand crafted models.}
elements.sent-2411-601: The X-axis represents SVI, and the Y-axis represents BVI.
elements.sent-2411-602: The iteration ranges shown on the plots indicate that all the benchmarks represented comparison points for all benchmarks with iterations within the indicated range.
